title: Offer & Sales Page Prompt Studio
kicker: Guided prompt system for conversion copy
subtitle: For owners, marketers, and copywriters turning an offer into a page that sells it. Every workflow builds a prompt that produces sales copy, page structure, or a fix list you can use the same day.

profile.label: What you sell, the price, and who buys it
profile.placeholder: Example: $89/month meal-planning app for shift workers, sold through a long-form sales page
profile.helper: One or two sentences. Every prompt in this studio uses this.

profile.label: Your buyer in their own words
profile.placeholder: Example: I know what to cook, I just have zero decision energy left after a 12-hour shift
profile.helper: Paste a real quote from a review, call, or DM if you have one.

profile.label: The page or offer you're working on right now
profile.placeholder: Example: rewriting the sales page for our annual plan before the January price increase

profile.label: What this page has to do in the next 90 days
profile.placeholder: Example: lift sales-page conversion from 1.1% to 2% on cold traffic

profile.label: Voice or constraints the copy has to respect
profile.placeholder: Example: plainspoken, no hype, we never discount and can't promise medical outcomes

categories.title: Offer Foundation
categories.note: Decide what you're selling and why it wins before a headline gets written.

categories.title: Buyer Evidence
categories.note: Map what your buyer wants, fears, says out loud, and already tried.

categories.title: Page Architecture
categories.note: Plan the page section by section and write the blocks that carry the argument.

categories.title: Proof and Believability
categories.note: Turn what you can actually back up into the most believed copy on the page.

categories.title: Price and Close
categories.note: Present the price, stack the value honestly, and walk the reader through checkout.

categories.title: Audit and Rewrite
categories.note: Find what's costing conversions on a live page and fix it in the right order.

workflows.title: Offer Direction Memo
workflows.description: Pins down the promise, the transformation, the conversion goal, and the honest reason to buy now before anyone writes a headline.
workflows.role: a direct-response offer strategist who plans sales pages for paid traffic and gets judged on conversion rate, not on how clever the copy sounds

fields.label: The change your buyer gets
fields.placeholder: Example: dinner decided by 4pm every day instead of a 9pm drive-thru run

fields.label: The one action the page asks for
fields.placeholder: Example: start the $1 fourteen-day trial

task: State the core promise in one sentence a stranger would understand on first read.
Describe the before and after in the buyer's terms: what a normal Tuesday looks like on each side of the purchase.
Name the single conversion action the page is built around and why a softer ask would leak sales.
Write the honest reason to buy now, using only real constraints from my context; if there isn't one, say so.
List the three claims this page will have to prove and what kind of proof each needs.
rules: If the promise depends on a result I haven't verified with real customers, mark it as unproven and suggest the closest claim I can back up.
workflows.output: a one-page offer direction memo with sections for promise, transformation, conversion action, reason to act, and claims that need proof

workflows.title: One-Line Offer Bank
workflows.description: Writes the single-sentence version of your offer, tuned separately for the page header, an ad, an email, and a spoken pitch.
workflows.role: a positioning writer who compresses offers into one sentence for a living and cuts any word the buyer wouldn't use themselves

fields.label: What's true about your offer that competitors can't say
fields.placeholder: Example: menus built around night-shift schedules, not a 6pm family dinner

task: Write five one-line versions of the offer, each built on a different anchor: outcome, mechanism, buyer identity, speed, or what it replaces.
Adapt the strongest line for four placements: sales-page header, paid ad, email subject plus first line, and how I'd say it out loud at an event.
For each placement, note the one word doing the most work and what breaks if it gets cut.
Flag any version that promises something my context doesn't support.
rules: Every line names the buyer or the outcome. Cut any line that could sit on a competitor's homepage without edits.
workflows.output: a table of one-line offer statements by placement, with the recommended pick marked and a one-line reason for it

workflows.title: Unique Mechanism Brief
workflows.description: Finds and names the thing inside your offer that explains why it works when the fixes your buyer already tried didn't.
workflows.role: a direct-response strategist who builds mechanism arguments for skeptical markets and treats an unnamed mechanism as an unfinished offer

fields.label: How your offer actually gets the result
fields.placeholder: Example: we plan meals around the buyer's actual work roster, pulled from their calendar

fields.label: What your buyer tried before you
fields.placeholder: Example: meal kits that assume you cook at 6pm, Pinterest boards, willpower

task: Identify the step, structure, or ingredient in my process that past solutions were missing.
Explain in plain language why that missing piece caused the old solutions to fail for this buyer.
Propose three names for the mechanism that describe what it does rather than sounding like a trademark filing.
Write a short mechanism paragraph for the sales page: the problem with the old way, the missing piece, how my offer supplies it.
List the proof I'd need to make the mechanism claim stick, based only on assets I mentioned.
rules: The mechanism must be something my offer genuinely does. If my context doesn't show one, say the offer needs a process difference before it needs a name.
workflows.output: a mechanism brief with the named mechanism, the failure story of prior solutions, a page-ready paragraph, and the proof list

workflows.title: Value Proposition Layers
workflows.description: Builds your value case on five levels, from practical result to emotional payoff to lowered risk, so the page can sell more than the feature list.
workflows.role: a conversion copywriter who maps value propositions for considered purchases and refuses to let a page lean on features alone

fields.label: What's included, in plain terms
fields.placeholder: Example: weekly roster-based menus, 15-minute recipes, a grocery list that autofills, cancel anytime

task: Translate each included item into the practical result the buyer gets from it.
Write the emotional layer: what the buyer stops feeling and starts feeling once the practical result shows up.
Write the confidence layer: why this will work for them specifically, given who they are.
Write the speed and risk layers: how fast the first result arrives and what protects them if it doesn't.
Mark which layer should lead the page for this buyer and which belongs near the price.
rules: Each layer gets its own sentence in buyer language. No layer may restate another one with fancier words.
workflows.output: a five-layer value map covering practical, emotional, confidence, speed, and risk, with a page-placement recommendation for each layer

workflows.title: Positioning Gap Map
workflows.description: Compares your offer against the alternatives your buyer actually weighs and finds the claim nobody else is making.
workflows.role: a competitive positioning analyst who reads competitor pages the way buyers do and hunts for the sentence no rival can copy

fields.label: The alternatives your buyer considers, including doing nothing
fields.placeholder: Example: HelloFresh, a $12 takeout habit, a laminated meal plan from a dietitian in 2023

fields.label: What those alternatives promise, roughly
fields.placeholder: Example: HelloFresh sells variety and convenience; takeout sells zero effort

task: List the promises the alternatives make and group them into the two or three positions already crowded in this market.
Identify what my offer does that no listed alternative claims, drawing only from my context.
Write the positioning statement that occupies that open ground in one sentence.
Stress-test it: name the alternative most likely to copy the claim and what would keep the copy from sticking.
rules: Positions must come from the alternatives I listed, not from a market you imagine around them.
workflows.output: a positioning map showing crowded claims, the open gap, a one-sentence position, and the defense note

workflows.title: Angle Bench
workflows.description: Generates ten distinct selling angles for your offer and ranks them by how much proof each one demands from you.
workflows.role: a direct-response creative lead who briefs sales-page tests and kills angles that need proof the client doesn't own

fields.label: Your strongest customer story or result so far
fields.placeholder: Example: an ER nurse who stopped spending $340 a month on hospital cafeteria food

task: Write ten angles, each anchored differently: pain, cost of waiting, identity, contrarian take, story, mechanism, comparison, speed, social norm, or math.
For each angle, write the opening line a sales page built on it would use.
Rank the ten by proof burden: what I'd need to show for the angle to hold, using the assets in my context.
Recommend the lead angle and one backup, with a sentence on which buyer each pulls hardest.
rules: Angles must differ in approach, not wording. If two angles could open the same page, merge them and write a new one.
workflows.output: a ranked bench of ten angles with opening lines, proof-burden notes, and a marked lead and backup

workflows.title: Offer Strength Scorecard
workflows.description: Scores your offer on the six things a cold reader silently checks before the copy gets a chance to matter.
workflows.role: an offer reviewer who audits promotions before launch budgets get spent and would rather fail an offer on paper than on traffic

fields.label: Price, guarantee, and terms as they stand today
fields.placeholder: Example: $89/month, cancel anytime, no refund on partial months, no trial

task: Score the offer from 1 to 5 on promise clarity, believability, urgency honesty, price logic, risk reversal, and ease of saying yes.
For each score under 4, name the exact change that would raise it and what the change costs me.
Identify the single weakest element and say whether copy can compensate for it or the offer itself has to change.
State plainly: write the page now, or fix the offer first.
rules: Score as a skeptical stranger, not as someone rooting for the offer. A 5 needs a stated reason.
workflows.output: a six-line scorecard with scores, fixes, what each fix costs, and a build-or-fix verdict

workflows.title: Reason to Act
workflows.description: Builds the honest answer to why now from real constraints you already have, and tells you straight when the page doesn't have one.
workflows.role: a promotions strategist who builds deadline logic for offers and treats a fake countdown timer as a refund generator

fields.label: Real constraints on your offer, if any
fields.placeholder: Example: price goes to $99 on March 1, we onboard 30 new accounts a month, bonus workshop happens live on the 12th

task: Sort my constraints into ones that create genuine time pressure and ones that only sound like they do.
Write the reason-to-act copy for the strongest real constraint: what changes, when, and what the reader loses by waiting.
Show where the reason appears on the page: near the fold, at the price, and in the close, with a one-line version for each spot.
If nothing I listed creates real pressure, say the page has no honest urgency and suggest which constraint I could create for real, like a cohort start date.
rules: Never manufacture a deadline, a stock limit, or a closing date I didn't give you. An offer with no urgency gets told so directly.
workflows.output: urgency copy for three page placements built on one real constraint, or a straight verdict that no honest urgency exists yet

workflows.title: Buyer Desire Map
workflows.description: Maps what your buyer says they want, what they actually want, who they're trying to become, and what they fear this purchase says about them.
workflows.role: a customer research analyst for direct-response offers who separates what buyers say on surveys from what they type at 1am

fields.label: Everything you know about this buyer
fields.placeholder: Example: 28-45, hospital and warehouse shift workers, tired of apps built for 9-to-5 families

task: Write the surface want: the request the buyer would state out loud.
Write the driving want underneath it: the felt problem that actually opens the wallet.
Describe the identity move: who the buyer becomes if this works, and who they fear staying.
List the fears attached to buying, including what a failed purchase would prove about them.
Name the trigger moments that push this buyer from tolerating the problem to searching for a fix.
rules: Ground every layer in the buyer detail I gave. Where you infer, mark the line with (inferred) so I know what to verify.
workflows.output: a desire map with surface want, driving want, identity move, fears, and trigger moments, each in the buyer's own vocabulary

workflows.title: Customer Language Mine
workflows.description: Pulls headline-ready phrases, objections, and desire statements out of raw reviews, survey answers, or call notes you paste in.
workflows.role: a voice-of-customer researcher who codes interview transcripts for copy teams and believes the best line on a sales page was said by a customer first

fields.label: Paste raw customer language
fields.placeholder: Example: paste 10-50 reviews, survey answers, support emails, or sales-call excerpts

task: Extract every phrase that describes the problem, the desired result, or a hesitation, keeping the customer's exact wording.
Group the phrases into themes and rank themes by how often they appear.
Mark the ten phrases with the most headline potential and say where each could sit: headline, crosshead, bullet, or FAQ.
List the objections that appear in the material, quoted directly.
Note what the material never mentions that I probably assume matters.
rules: Keep quotes verbatim, typos included. Paraphrase kills the evidence.
workflows.output: a voice-of-customer report with ranked themes, ten placed headline candidates, quoted objections, and the list of things customers never mention

workflows.title: Awareness Stage Map
workflows.description: Sorts your traffic into the five awareness stages and writes the first thing the page must say to each one.
workflows.role: a funnel copywriter who matches message to awareness stage and has watched solution-aware copy die in front of problem-aware traffic

fields.label: Where readers of this page come from
fields.placeholder: Example: cold Facebook ads about late-night takeout guilt, plus a 2,000-person email list

task: Estimate how my traffic splits across unaware, problem-aware, solution-aware, product-aware, and most-aware, based on the sources I named.
For the two biggest stages, write the opening line the page needs so that reader keeps going.
State which stage this one page should be built for and how to bridge the others in, or whether a stage needs its own page.
List the copy elements each chosen stage requires before it will believe the offer: education, comparison, proof, or just the deal.
rules: Tie every stage estimate to a traffic source I named. No stage gets copy advice without a reason it shows up in my traffic.
workflows.output: an awareness breakdown with rough percentages, stage-matched opening lines, a primary-stage decision, and required copy elements per stage

workflows.title: Objection Inventory
workflows.description: Collects every reason your buyer says no, finds the belief underneath each one, and ranks them by how many sales they block.
workflows.role: a sales-page strategist who builds objection lists from real buyer behavior and knows the loudest objection is rarely the one killing conversions

fields.label: Objections you've heard, word for word if possible
fields.placeholder: Example: I'll just cancel and forget, another subscription, my schedule changes weekly so plans never survive

fields.label: Where people drop off or go silent
fields.placeholder: Example: 60% scroll past the price and leave without clicking FAQ

task: List every objection from my input plus the unstated ones this offer type always carries, marked as assumed.
For each, name the belief underneath it: what the buyer would have to accept for the objection to dissolve.
Rank the list by estimated sales blocked, using my drop-off signals where I gave them.
For the top three, write the one-sentence answer the page should make, and note whether it needs proof, a guarantee, or just clearer copy.
rules: Answer objections with facts from my context. Where the honest answer is weak, say the offer needs to change instead of the copy.
workflows.output: a ranked objection table with the underlying belief, the page's answer, and what backs each answer up

workflows.title: Buying Trigger Timeline
workflows.description: Reconstructs the week your buyer goes from putting up with the problem to paying for a fix, so the page can meet them mid-search.
workflows.role: a buyer-journey researcher who traces the events before a purchase and plans copy around the moment the search actually starts

fields.label: What you think sets off the purchase
fields.placeholder: Example: third week of night shifts, jeans feel tight, partner comments on the takeout bags

task: Lay out the trigger sequence as a timeline: the buildup, the tipping event, the first search, and the comparison window.
For each timeline point, write what the buyer is feeling and the exact kind of message that would land there.
Identify which point my page traffic arrives at and what that means for how fast the page must name the problem.
Write the two opening lines that meet the buyer at that point, one leading with the tipping event, one with the outcome.
rules: Build the timeline from my trigger guess and buyer context, and flag any step you added on inference.
workflows.output: a four-point trigger timeline with feelings, matched messages, an arrival-point call, and two opening lines to test

workflows.title: Failed Fixes Ledger
workflows.description: Documents everything your buyer already tried, why each attempt fell apart, and how the page uses that history without insulting them.
workflows.role: a sales-message strategist who builds not-your-fault arguments from real failure patterns instead of flattery

fields.label: What your buyers tried before finding you
fields.placeholder: Example: meal kits (portions wrong for one person), Sunday batch cooking (dead by Wednesday), a nutrition coach at $200/month

task: For each past attempt, name the structural reason it failed for this buyer, not a willpower reason.
Find the common thread across the failures: the wrong assumption every old solution shared.
Write the page paragraph that retells this history in second person, ending where my mechanism enters.
List the phrases to avoid because they'd make the reader feel stupid for having tried those fixes.
rules: Failures get explained by design flaws in the old solutions. The reader is never the reason the old thing failed.
workflows.output: a failure ledger with structural reasons, the shared wrong assumption, a page-ready paragraph, and a list of phrases to avoid

workflows.title: Toughest Skeptic Profile
workflows.description: Profiles the hardest reader your page will face and lists the exact evidence that would move them from arms crossed to card out.
workflows.role: a conversion strategist who writes for the skeptic in the audience, because copy that converts doubters converts everyone above them

fields.label: Your most doubtful buyer and why they doubt
fields.placeholder: Example: a nurse who bought two meal apps already, canceled both by week three, saw no roster feature in either

task: Write the skeptic's internal monologue as they hit the page: their first three thoughts, in their voice.
List the claims on a typical page for this offer that the skeptic dismisses instantly and why.
Specify the evidence that would actually register: what kind of demo, number, or story, drawn from my available assets.
Write the one section this page needs specifically for the skeptic and say where it goes.
rules: If my assets can't satisfy the skeptic on a claim, say which claim to drop rather than which adjective to add.
workflows.output: a skeptic profile with the monologue, dismissed claims, an evidence spec, and one page section written for them with placement

workflows.title: Primary Reader Decision
workflows.description: Forces the call on which single buyer this page speaks to when several segments could plausibly buy.
workflows.role: a messaging strategist who makes one-reader decisions for pages that were converting nobody by addressing everybody

fields.label: The buyer groups who could buy this
fields.placeholder: Example: night-shift nurses, warehouse leads on rotating schedules, new dads doing 5am starts

fields.label: What you know about each group's buying so far
fields.placeholder: Example: nurses reply to ads most, warehouse buyers churn least, dads came from one podcast

task: Compare the segments on desire intensity, ability to pay, reachability, and how well my current proof fits each.
Recommend the primary reader and defend the call in three sentences using my signals.
Show what the headline, hero image direction, and first testimonial slot look like once that reader is chosen.
Explain how the other segments still buy from a page not aimed at them, and where a secondary nod can sit without diluting the lead.
rules: Pick one reader. A recommendation to target two segments equally counts as refusing the assignment.
workflows.output: a one-reader decision memo with the comparison, the call, three page changes it drives, and the secondary-segment note

workflows.title: Sales Page Blueprint
workflows.description: Plans your page section by section, with the job, the message, and the proof each section carries before any copy gets drafted.
workflows.role: a sales-page architect who storyboards long-form pages for direct offers and assigns every section one job it can be fired for missing

fields.label: Rough length you're planning
Proof and assets you can place on the page
fields.placeholder: Example: 14 written reviews, one 90-second demo video, a before-and-after grocery bill

task: Recommend the section order for this offer and traffic, naming each section and its single job.
For each section, write the core message in one sentence and assign a proof asset from my list where one fits.
Mark the three sections where readers most often leave for this kind of offer and what keeps them moving.
Flag sections that common templates include but this page should skip, with the reason.
If I chose not sure on length, make the length call from my price and traffic context.
rules: Every proof asset placed must come from my list. Sections without an available proof asset get labeled claim-only so I can see the risk.
workflows.output: a numbered section blueprint with each section's job, message, assigned proof, exit-risk flags, and skipped-section notes

workflows.title: Above-the-Fold Block
workflows.description: Writes the headline, subhead, first call to action, and hero proof line that decide whether anyone reads section two.
workflows.role: a direct-response copywriter who writes page openings for cold traffic, where the fold gets five seconds to earn the scroll

fields.label: The angle this page leads with
fields.placeholder: Example: dinner decided by 4pm no matter what shift you're on

fields.label: The strongest proof you can show at the top
fields.placeholder: Example: 4.8 stars from 212 shift workers

task: Write three headline options on my angle: one outcome-led, one specificity-led, one pain-led.
Write the subhead for each, adding the mechanism or the who-this-is-for that the headline left out.
Write the first CTA button copy plus the reassurance line under it.
Place my hero proof and write its one-line caption; if I gave none, mark the slot empty rather than filling it.
Recommend which of the three blocks to run first and summarize what a stranger takes from a five-second read of it.
rules: The fold must tell a stranger what this is, who it's for, and why to care without scrolling. If a headline needs the body copy to make sense, rewrite it.
workflows.output: three complete fold blocks with headline, subhead, CTA, and proof caption, plus a recommended pick and its five-second read summary

workflows.title: Headline Bench
workflows.description: Produces fifteen headlines across five styles and tells you which three earn a test slot and why the rest don't.
workflows.role: a headline specialist who writes and ranks headline slates for conversion tests and scores clever below clear every time

fields.label: The claim the headline must carry
fields.placeholder: Example: shift workers save 5 hours and about $200 a month on food decisions

task: Write three headlines each in five styles: outcome, specificity, authority, pain, and curiosity.
Score each headline on clarity, believability, and pull for my primary reader, 1 to 5 each.
Pick the top three for testing and write the subhead each would need.
Point out the headline pattern in this batch most likely to get ignored by this audience and why.
rules: Curiosity headlines must still name a real subject. A headline that could tease any product gets cut before scoring.
workflows.output: a fifteen-headline bench grouped by style with scores, three test picks with subheads, and the pattern warning

workflows.title: Lead Story Arc
workflows.description: Structures the opening story of your page, the beats, the turn, and the handoff into the offer, from a story you actually have.
workflows.role: a sales-letter writer who opens pages with story leads and cuts any beat that delays the reader's first yes

fields.label: The true story this page could open with
fields.placeholder: Example: our founder fell asleep in a Wendy's drive-thru after a double shift, and that's the night the roster feature got sketched

fields.label: Whose story this tells
options: A composite that needs flagging as illustrative

task: Break my story into beats: the moment, the struggle, the turn, and the discovery, cutting everything that doesn't serve the offer.
Write the first 150 words of the lead using those beats, in first or second person depending on whose story it is.
Write the pivot sentence that hands the story to the reader: this is where their version of the story changes.
Note where the story should get a callback later on the page, at the guarantee or the close.
rules: Use only the events I gave you. If the story is a composite, the copy must say so on the page.
workflows.output: a story-beat outline, a 150-word lead draft, the pivot sentence, and one callback placement

workflows.title: Crosshead Skim Path
workflows.description: Writes the crossheads so a skimmer who reads nothing else still gets the full argument and reaches the price ready to buy.
workflows.role: a long-form page editor who tests pages by reading crossheads only, because a third of buyers do exactly that

fields.label: Your page sections in order
fields.placeholder: Example: hero, problem, story, mechanism, product, testimonials, price, guarantee, FAQ, close

task: Write one crosshead per section that advances the argument instead of labeling the section, so no crosshead reads like a category name.
Read the crossheads back as a sequence and confirm they form a complete case: problem, cause, fix, proof, deal, safety, ask.
Fix any spot in the sequence where a skimmer would lose the thread or be asked to buy before the case is made.
Mark the two crossheads that carry the most weight and give one alternate for each.
rules: Every crosshead makes a statement or a promise. Section labels and single-word headings don't count as crossheads.
workflows.output: a full crosshead set in page order, a paraphrase of what the skim-read alone communicates, and alternates for the two heaviest

workflows.title: CTA Block Bank
workflows.description: Writes every call-to-action block on the page, button copy, surrounding lines, and reassurance, matched to how warm the reader is at that scroll depth.
workflows.role: a conversion copywriter who writes CTA sections for long pages and matches the ask to how much of the case the reader has seen so far

fields.label: What clicking actually does
fields.placeholder: Example: goes to a Stripe checkout, $89/month, card required

fields.label: How many CTA blocks the page has

task: Write each CTA block for its position: early blocks assume less conviction, late blocks ask plainly.
For each block, write the button copy, the lead-in line above it, and the reassurance line below it.
Vary the button copy across blocks without getting cute: the reader always knows what happens on click.
Add the friction warning: anything in my checkout action, like card required, that the copy near the button should say before the click does.
rules: Button copy describes the action or the outcome, never Submit or Learn more. The reassurance line states a fact, not a feeling.
workflows.output: a numbered set of CTA blocks in page order, each with lead-in, button copy, reassurance line, and the friction disclosure

workflows.title: FAQ Objection Section
workflows.description: Turns your real buyer questions into an FAQ that closes sales instead of parking legal boilerplate at the bottom of the page.
workflows.role: a sales copywriter who treats the FAQ as the page's second close, because hesitant buyers scroll there before they decide

fields.label: Questions buyers actually ask
fields.placeholder: Example: what happens when my roster changes mid-week, can I pause during vacation, is this just another meal-kit thing

task: Order my questions by buying impact, opening with the one that blocks the most sales.
Write each answer to resolve the worry, state the honest limitation if there is one, and end pointed at the purchase.
Add up to three questions buyers think but rarely type for this kind of offer, marked as added, with answers.
Identify any question whose honest answer hurts, and recommend fixing the offer term versus wording around it.
rules: Answers state facts from my context. An answer that dodges the question gets replaced with the honest version, even when it's less flattering.
workflows.output: an ordered FAQ section with impact-ranked questions, honest answers that end toward purchase, and flags where the offer itself needs work

workflows.title: Page Length Verdict
workflows.description: Decides how long this page should be and what format fits, based on your price, traffic temperature, and how much proof you own.
workflows.role: a conversion consultant who sizes pages against offers and has cut as many bloated letters as he's extended thin ones

fields.label: Traffic temperature hitting this page
How much proof you own
options: Moderate: reviews plus one strong story

task: Make the length call in words and sections, from my price, traffic temperature, and proof depth.
Explain the two factors that drove the call and what change would flip it.
Recommend the format: letter, hybrid page with modules, or short page plus video, and say what my proof depth rules out.
List what to cut first if the page comes in long, and what never gets cut at this price point.
rules: One verdict with numbers, not a range wide enough to dodge the call. Hedging across two formats counts as no answer.
workflows.output: a length and format verdict with a word count, section count, the two driving factors, and the cut-first list

workflows.title: Proof Asset Inventory
workflows.description: Catalogs every piece of proof you own, grades each one's believability, and shows which claims on your page still stand bare.
workflows.role: a copy chief who audits proof before drafts get written and won't let a page make a claim its evidence file can't cash

fields.label: Every proof item you have, big or small
fields.placeholder: Example: 212 app-store reviews averaging 4.8, one nurse's grocery receipts, a screenshot of our churn dashboard

fields.label: The main claims your page makes or will make
fields.placeholder: Example: saves 5 hours a week, works with rotating rosters, cheaper than takeout

task: Catalog my proof items by type: numbers, testimonials, demonstrations, credentials, third-party mentions.
Grade each item's believability to a skeptical stranger and note what would raise the grade, like adding a name, date, or source.
Match proof to each claim I listed and mark every claim left unsupported.
For each bare claim, state the options: gather specific new proof, soften the claim, or cut it.
rules: Work only with proof I listed. Never suggest inventing, projecting, or rounding a number to fill a gap.
workflows.output: a graded proof inventory, a claim-to-proof match table, and a decision list for every unsupported claim

workflows.title: Testimonial Shaping Pass
workflows.description: Edits the raw customer quotes you paste in for length and placement while keeping every word theirs, then slots each one where it sells.
workflows.role: a conversion editor who curates testimonial sections and knows a specific mediocre quote outsells a glowing vague one

fields.label: Paste your raw testimonials or reviews
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the full text of 5-30 reviews or quotes, with names or initials where you have permission

task: Pick the quotes worth using and say what each proves: a result, an objection answered, a buyer type represented.
Trim each keeper to its selling core by cutting only, never rewording, and show the before and after.
Assign each quote a page placement next to the claim it supports, so no testimonial sits in a generic wall.
Write the short bold lead-in line above each quote, drawn from the quote's own words.
List what's missing from the set, like no skeptic-turned-buyer quote, so I know what to request from customers next.
rules: Cutting is allowed, rewording isn't. If a quote needs new words to work, it doesn't get used.
workflows.output: a placed testimonial set with trims shown, lead-in lines, the claim each quote supports, and a request list for missing quote types

workflows.title: Guarantee Design
workflows.description: Designs the guarantee your economics can survive and writes it so the reader feels the risk move from their side of the table to yours.
workflows.role: a risk-reversal strategist who designs guarantees for paid offers and checks the refund math before writing a word of promise

fields.label: Your current refund or guarantee terms
fields.placeholder: Example: 14-day refund on request, about 3% of buyers use it

fields.label: What a refund actually costs you
fields.placeholder: Example: about $11 in onboarding time plus the Stripe fee

task: Assess my current terms: what they promise, what they signal, and where they leave the buyer still holding risk.
Design two stronger structures I could afford given my refund cost and usage rate, like a longer window, keep-the-bonus, or a conditional results guarantee.
Write the guarantee copy for the recommended structure with a plain name, what the buyer does to claim it, and zero weasel words.
Show the placements: the guarantee section, the one-liner near the price, and the mention inside the close.
rules: Every structure must be one I can honor at my stated refund cost. A guarantee with hidden conditions gets treated as worse than a short honest one.
workflows.output: a guarantee assessment, two affordable structures with a recommendation, final guarantee copy, and three placements

workflows.title: Credibility Block
workflows.description: Writes the who-are-you section from your real background so a stranger trusts the source without reading a single brag.
workflows.role: a bio writer for sales pages who builds authority from relevant specifics and cuts any line that exists to impress rather than reassure

fields.label: Your actual background, the relevant bits
fields.placeholder: Example: 11 years as a hospital dietitian, built the first menus on night shift in the break room, no culinary degree

task: Pick the two or three facts from my background that answer the reader's real question: why would this person's solution work for me?
Write the credibility block in 80 to 120 words, first person, leading with the fact most relevant to the buyer's problem.
Cut or rephrase anything that reads as status display rather than relevance, and show what got cut and why.
Write the one-line version for the page footer or an ad.
rules: Relevance beats prestige: a fact that connects to the buyer's problem outranks a bigger fact that doesn't. Missing credentials get acknowledged, not hidden, when the reader will wonder.
workflows.output: a credibility block of 80 to 120 words, the cut list with reasons, and a one-line footer version

workflows.title: Claim Support Audit
workflows.description: Goes through your pasted page line by line and marks every claim as proven, provable, or naked, so you fix believability before traffic finds the holes.
workflows.role: a compliance-minded copy chief who reads pages the way a doubtful buyer and a regulator would, in that order

fields.label: Paste the page or section to audit
fields.placeholder: Example: paste your full sales page or the section making the heaviest claims

task: Extract every factual claim from the pasted copy, including implied ones like photos suggesting results.
Mark each claim proven (evidence shown on the page), provable (I likely have evidence that isn't shown), or naked (no support in sight).
For provable claims, say exactly what to add and where; for naked ones, write the softened version that stays honest or recommend the cut.
Flag any claim that could draw a complaint or a platform rejection for this offer type.
rules: Judge only what's on the page. A claim I could prove but didn't show still gets marked, because the reader can't see my files.
workflows.output: a claim-by-claim audit table with proven, provable, or naked status, the fix per claim, and a risk-flag list

workflows.title: Demonstration Concepts
workflows.description: Designs ways to show the product working on the page, in numbers, screenshots, or short video, so the copy can point instead of insist.
workflows.role: a product marketer who builds show-not-tell moments for sales pages and trades three adjectives for one screenshot every time

fields.label: What you could realistically capture or show
fields.placeholder: Example: a screen recording of a menu regenerating after a roster change, a real grocery bill before and after

task: Rank my showable items by how directly each one proves the core promise.
For the top three, design the demonstration: what gets captured, how long it runs, and the caption that tells the reader what they're seeing.
Place each demonstration on the page next to the claim it replaces or supports.
Write the fallback for my strongest claim if none of my showable items covers it: the most concrete verbal proof available from my context.
rules: Demonstrations use real product behavior and real numbers only. A mocked-up result is a fabricated testimonial with extra steps.
workflows.output: three designed demonstrations with capture specs, captions, and page placements, plus one verbal fallback

workflows.title: Numbers That Hold
workflows.description: Takes the real figures you supply and presents each one with the context, source, and precision that make a skeptic accept it.
workflows.role: a direct-response editor who handles statistics on sales pages and knows a sourced 4.6 beats an unsourced 5.0

fields.label: Your real numbers, with where each comes from
fields.placeholder: Example: 212 reviews at 4.8 (App Store, pulled May 3), average member logs 11 meals/week (internal dashboard)

task: For each figure, write the page-ready presentation: the number, its source, and its date or sample size, in one line.
Flag figures a skeptic would challenge and adjust the framing to what the source honestly supports, like members who log meals instead of all members.
Rank the figures by persuasive weight for my offer and pick the one that belongs above the fold.
List the numbers my page wants but I don't have, and which one is worth measuring first.
rules: Present only figures I supplied, at the precision I supplied them. Rounding up, projecting forward, or dropping the source note is off the table.
workflows.output: page-ready one-liners for every figure, skeptic flags with honest reframes, a ranked list, and a numbers wishlist

workflows.title: Case Story Builder
workflows.description: Turns one customer's real result into a short named story with a beginning, a number, and a turn, ready to sit beside your boldest claim.
workflows.role: a case-study writer for conversion pages who keeps stories under 200 words because buyers read proof, not literature

fields.label: The customer facts you have permission to use
fields.placeholder: Example: Dana M., ICU nurse, cut food spend from $610 to $370/month in 8 weeks, said she stopped dreading 6pm

fields.label: The page claim this story should support
fields.placeholder: Example: cheaper than the takeout habit it replaces

task: Structure the facts into a story arc: where the customer started, what they did with my offer, and what changed, with the number as the turn.
Write the story in under 200 words using only my supplied facts and quotes.
Write the headline for the story block that leads with the specific result.
Place the story next to the claim it supports and note whether it needs a photo, first name, or role line to land, based on my stated permission.
rules: Every fact, name, and quote comes from my input, used within the permission I stated. A gap in the story stays a gap; never bridge it with an invented detail.
workflows.output: a case story under 200 words with a headline, its placement next to the claim, and a presentation note based on permissions

workflows.title: Pricing Story Frame
workflows.description: Builds the argument that makes your price feel like the reasonable option, anchored to what the problem already costs the buyer.
workflows.role: a pricing copy strategist who frames prices against the buyer's current spending, because a number standing alone always feels expensive

fields.label: Your price and billing terms
fields.placeholder: Example: $89/month, or $890/year with two months free

fields.label: What the problem costs your buyer now, if you know
fields.placeholder: Example: members report $300-500/month on takeout and delivery fees before joining

task: Choose the anchor for my price: the cost of the problem, the price of alternatives, or the value of the outcome, using whichever my context supports with real numbers.
Write the pricing narrative: two or three paragraphs that walk from anchor to price, so the price arrives as the smaller number.
Write the price-reveal line itself and what sits immediately after it: the guarantee mention or the strongest reassurance I have.
If my context gives no honest anchor number, say so and frame the price on terms transparency instead: what's included, what it replaces, and when they can leave.
rules: Anchors use numbers from my context only. Comparing to a made-up competitor charging $500 is fabrication, not framing.
workflows.output: a pricing narrative of two to three paragraphs, the price-reveal line with its follow-up, and the anchor logic stated plainly

workflows.title: Bonus Stack Logic
workflows.description: Picks bonuses that each dissolve a named objection instead of padding the offer with digital extras nobody redeems.
workflows.role: an offer builder who assembles bonus stacks by objection and cuts any bonus that exists just to raise the total-value number

fields.label: Bonuses you could actually deliver
fields.placeholder: Example: a 15-minute recipe pack, a live roster-setup call, a printable freezer guide

fields.label: The objections still standing at the price
fields.placeholder: Example: my schedule changes weekly, I don't want another app to learn

task: Match each possible bonus to the objection it dissolves, and cut bonuses that map to no objection.
Order the surviving stack from broadest objection to narrowest.
Write the copy block for each bonus: name, what it does, and the objection-tied reason it's included.
Assign honest value figures only where I charge those prices elsewhere; otherwise present bonuses without price tags.
Say whether the stack needs one more bonus and exactly which objection it should target.
rules: A bonus that answers no listed objection gets cut even if it's impressive. Invented value price tags never appear unless I really sell the item at that price.
workflows.output: an objection-matched bonus stack with copy blocks, honest value treatment, and a gap recommendation

workflows.title: Deadline Copy Pass
workflows.description: Writes the countdown, deadline, and last-call copy for a real constraint you supply, and refuses the job when there isn't one.
workflows.role: a launch copywriter who writes deadline sequences for offers with real closes and walks away from evergreen pages dressed up as closing ones

fields.label: The real deadline or limit, exactly as it stands
fields.placeholder: Example: cart closes Sunday 11:59pm Pacific because onboarding calls start Monday

task: Verify the constraint is real and specific: a date, a capacity number, or a price change with a stated reason. If it's vague, stop and list what would make it real.
Write the deadline copy for three page locations: near the fold, at the price, and in the final close.
Write the reason-why sentence that explains the deadline, because unexplained deadlines read as fake.
Draft the last-24-hours variant for each location, tightening the copy without adding panic punctuation.
rules: No invented scarcity, no timers that reset, no almost-sold-out lines without a number behind them. If the constraint I gave is soft, deliver the not-ready verdict instead of softened copy.
workflows.output: deadline copy for three placements plus last-24-hours variants, each with the reason-why attached, or a verdict that the constraint isn't real yet

workflows.title: Objection Placement Map
workflows.description: Decides where on the page each objection gets answered, in the copy, the proof, the FAQ, or the guarantee, so nothing waits until the reader's already gone.
workflows.role: a page strategist who choreographs objection handling by scroll position, because an objection answered two sections late converts nobody

fields.label: Your objections, ranked if you can
fields.placeholder: Example: 1) price vs takeout, 2) will it survive my rotating roster, 3) another subscription to forget

task: Assign each objection its handling method: rewrite the claim, add proof beside it, an FAQ entry, a guarantee mention, or an offer change.
Place each answer at the scroll position where that objection first forms in the reader's head, and say why there.
Write the actual answer line or block for the top two objections.
Identify the one objection copy can't fix and what offer or product change it points to.
rules: An objection may be answered before the reader consciously has it, but never more than one section after. Late answers get moved, not duplicated.
workflows.output: an objection-to-placement map with methods and positions, written answers for the top two, and the copy-can't-fix flag

workflows.title: Final Close Section
workflows.description: Writes the last stretch of the page, the recap, the honest decision frame, the guarantee restated, and the final ask, without the melodrama endings that leak trust.
workflows.role: a sales-letter closer who writes final sections that summarize the case and make the ask plainly, then stop

fields.label: The three strongest points your page made
fields.placeholder: Example: menus follow your roster, 212 nurses rated it 4.8, costs less than two takeout nights

task: Write the recap paragraph that restates my three points as things the reader now knows, not as a feature list.
Frame the decision honestly: what saying yes starts, and what waiting costs in the buyer's terms from my context.
Restate the guarantee in one sentence and make the final ask with the exact button action named.
Run a cut check: remove any line in the close that raises a new claim, because new claims this late create new doubts.
rules: No two-roads theatrics and no final guilt push. The close may only use points the page already proved.
workflows.output: a complete closing section: recap paragraph, honest decision frame, guarantee line, and final CTA block

workflows.title: Checkout Confidence Pass
workflows.description: Writes the order-page copy, field labels, reassurance lines, and the summary box, so the sale survives the last click.
workflows.role: a checkout optimizer who treats the order page as the spot most likely to lose a decided buyer over one stray doubt

fields.label: What your checkout shows and asks for
fields.placeholder: Example: Stripe page, card only, shows $89/month, no order summary, no guarantee mention

task: List what my checkout currently communicates versus what a nervous buyer needs at this step: what they get, what they pay and when, and how they leave.
Write the order summary box copy: offer name, what's included in shorthand, price with billing terms, and the guarantee one-liner.
Write the reassurance micro-copy: under the card field, next to the buy button, and the line that answers the biggest known objection one last time.
Flag anything in my described checkout that contradicts the sales page, like a price shown differently.
rules: Nothing new gets sold at checkout. Every line either confirms what the page promised or removes a doubt about the transaction itself.
workflows.output: a checkout copy kit: order summary box, three placed micro-copy lines, and a contradiction flag list

workflows.title: Payment Plan Framing
workflows.description: Decides how to present pay-in-full against installments using your real prices, and writes the copy for whichever split wins.
workflows.role: a pricing presentation specialist who structures payment choices so the buyer picks fast instead of leaving to think about the math

fields.label: The payment options you can offer
fields.placeholder: Example: $890/year or $89/month; could do 3 payments of $315 if it helps

task: Lay out my options as the buyer sees them, with the true total of each spelled out.
Recommend which option to feature and how to order them, with reasoning tied to my price points and buyer.
Write the copy for the payment choice block: option names, the one-line case for each, and the default selection note.
Write the honest treatment of any difference, like two months free, only where my numbers actually produce it.
rules: Totals appear in full for every option; hiding an annual total behind a monthly figure is off the table. Discount language must match the arithmetic exactly.
workflows.output: a payment presentation block with the featured option, per-option copy lines, true totals, and the ordering rationale

workflows.title: Offer Recap Box
workflows.description: Writes the boxed summary beside your final CTA listing everything included, so the buyer's last look confirms the deal instead of raising questions.
workflows.role: an offer-presentation writer who builds the everything-you-get box and knows it gets read more carefully than the letter above it

fields.label: Everything the buyer gets, in your words
fields.placeholder: Example: weekly roster menus, grocery autolist, 15-minute recipe library, live setup call, freezer guide bonus

task: Rewrite each included item as the result it delivers, keeping the item name where buyers already know it.
Order the list with the core deliverable first and bonuses last, marked as bonuses.
Write the box header, the price line with terms, the guarantee one-liner, and the CTA that sits inside or directly under the box.
Run a cut check: flag any item so minor it makes the stack look padded, and say whether to merge or drop it.
rules: The box must scan in a few seconds: one line per item, no paragraph creep. Value price tags appear only if I gave real standalone prices.
workflows.output: a complete recap box: header, ordered one-line items, price line, guarantee line, CTA, plus the padding flags

workflows.title: Full Page Teardown
workflows.description: Reviews your pasted sales page section by section, scoring what each is supposed to do against what it actually does, and hands back a fix list in order of impact.
workflows.role: a conversion copy chief who tears down live pages for offer owners and reports what's costing money first, not what's easiest to praise

fields.label: Paste the full sales page
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the whole page top to bottom, headline through final CTA, button copy included

fields.label: Any performance numbers you have
fields.placeholder: Example: 1.2% conversion, 55% read past the fold, mostly cold traffic

task: Walk the page section by section: name each section's job and score how well the copy does that job, 1 to 5.
Identify the three highest-impact problems, using my performance numbers to locate the damage where I gave them.
For each of the three, quote the specific lines at fault and write the fixed version.
List the smaller issues as ranked one-liners so nothing found gets lost.
End with the single change to make first and the result it should move.
rules: Quote the page's own lines when criticizing them; no verdicts without the evidence shown. Rewrites keep my facts and claims exactly as stated, fixing delivery only.
workflows.output: a section-scored teardown, three deep-dive fixes with rewritten lines, a ranked minor-issue list, and the do-this-first call

workflows.title: Headline Rework Pass
workflows.description: Diagnoses why your pasted headline block isn't pulling and rebuilds it three ways while keeping every factual claim you made.
workflows.role: a headline doctor who rewrites underperforming page tops and starts from what the old headline failed to communicate, not from a blank page

fields.label: Paste your current headline, subhead, and any fold copy
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the exact fold text as it appears, including the button

task: Diagnose the current block: what a five-second stranger takes away, and which of clarity, believability, or relevance breaks first.
Rebuild it three ways, each keeping my factual claims: one clarity-first, one specificity-first, one buyer-identity-first.
For each rebuild, note what got cut from the original and why keeping it was costing comprehension.
Recommend which rebuild to test against the original and the response signal that would settle it.
rules: The rewrites may sharpen my claims but never enlarge them. If the original headline overclaims, say so before rebuilding smaller.
workflows.output: a five-second diagnosis, three rebuilt fold blocks with cut notes, and a test recommendation with its success signal

workflows.title: Conversion Leak Ranking
workflows.description: Predicts where readers stall or bail on your page, at the hero, the price, or the form, and ranks the leaks by the sales each one costs.
workflows.role: a CRO analyst who reads pages like scroll maps, predicting drop-off points from copy and layout before the heat map ever loads

fields.label: Paste the page copy in order
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the page text top to bottom so scroll position can be estimated

fields.label: Any behavior data you have
fields.placeholder: Example: average scroll 48%, price section heat is cold, FAQ gets 3x the clicks of the CTA

task: Mark the likely stall points on the pasted page: where attention dips, where a claim strains belief, where the next step goes unclear.
Cross-check the predictions against my behavior data where I gave it, and adjust.
Rank the leaks by estimated sales impact, stating the reasoning for each rank.
For the top two leaks, prescribe the fix: a copy change, a section move, or a cut, naming the specific lines involved.
rules: Every predicted leak points to specific lines or positions in my pasted copy. General theory about attention spans doesn't count as a finding.
workflows.output: a stall-point map keyed to my page, a ranked leak list with reasoning, and two prescribed fixes naming exact lines

workflows.title: A/B Test Queue
workflows.description: Turns your page's open questions into a ranked test queue, each with a hypothesis, a variant, and the number that decides it.
workflows.role: a conversion testing lead who runs one honest test at a time and ranks the queue by expected lift, not by which idea is most fun

fields.label: What you're considering testing
fields.placeholder: Example: headline vs new angle, $89 vs $79, moving testimonials above the price

fields.label: Monthly visitors and conversions
fields.placeholder: Example: about 6,000 visits, 70 sales a month

task: Turn each candidate into a hypothesis: if we change X, metric Y moves, because of Z from my buyer context.
Rank the queue by expected impact and by whether my traffic volume can actually resolve the test in under two months.
For the top test, define the variant precisely, the primary metric, and the rough sample it needs.
Flag any candidate too small to matter or too tangled to read cleanly, and say what to do with it instead.
rules: Tests my traffic can't resolve get named as such, not queued politely. One primary metric per test; secondary metrics are observations, not verdicts.
workflows.output: a ranked test queue with hypotheses, a fully specified first test with sample guidance, and the not-worth-testing list

workflows.title: Rewrite Priority List
workflows.description: Takes everything you know is wrong with the page and sequences the rewrite so the highest-revenue fixes ship first, not the easiest ones.
workflows.role: a copy project lead who triages page rewrites for owners with limited hours and defends the order against the temptation to polish

fields.label: Everything you suspect is wrong
fields.placeholder: Example: headline feels flat, no proof near the price, FAQ is legal boilerplate, close is weak

fields.label: Hours you can spend on this
fields.placeholder: Example: 6 hours over two weekends

task: Sort my issues by revenue impact, using the rule that problems before the price reveal usually outrank problems after it.
Estimate the effort per fix and build the sequence that fits my stated hours, biggest impact first.
For each scheduled fix, write the one-sentence brief: what changes, what it must preserve, and how to check it worked.
Name what deliberately stays unfixed this round and why that's the right call at my time budget.
rules: The sequence must respect my hours; a plan that assumes more time than I have is a wish, not a plan.
workflows.output: a sequenced rewrite plan with effort estimates, one-sentence briefs per fix, and the explicitly deferred list

workflows.title: Checkout Copy Audit
workflows.description: Reviews your pasted checkout or order page for the doubts it plants and the reassurances it forgets, then supplies the missing lines.
workflows.role: an order-page reviewer who has watched decided buyers abandon over one ambiguous billing line and audits checkouts for exactly those

fields.label: Paste your checkout page text
fields.placeholder: Example: paste every visible word on the order page, field labels and button text included

task: List what the pasted checkout communicates and what it leaves the buyer to wonder about: total charged today, renewal terms, how to cancel, and what happens after clicking.
Flag every line that plants doubt or contradicts a sales-page promise from my context.
Write the missing reassurance lines with exact placements: near the price, near the card fields, under the button.
Rank all findings by abandonment risk and mark the first fix.
rules: Audit only the pasted text; no assumptions about what the design shows. Fixes must state true terms from my context, never softer ones.
workflows.output: a checkout audit with the doubt list, contradiction flags, written reassurance lines with placements, and a ranked fix order

workflows.title: Message Match Check
workflows.description: Compares the ad or email that sends traffic against the page that receives it and pinpoints where the promise shifts enough to lose the click.
workflows.role: a funnel continuity reviewer who reads the ad and the page back to back the way a clicker experiences them, seconds apart

fields.label: Paste the ad or email, then the page copy below it
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the ad text, a divider line, then the landing page headline and first sections

task: Extract the promise, tone, and specific hooks from the ad or email portion.
Check the page's fold against each: whether the headline continues the promise, the tone holds, and the hook reappears.
Mark every mismatch with both lines quoted side by side and rate how jarring each is to a fresh clicker.
Rewrite the page fold or the ad, whichever is the cheaper fix, to restore the match, and say which side you chose to change and why.
rules: Match means continuity, not repetition; flag both broken promises and word-for-word duplication that wastes the fold. The rewrite changes one side only.
workflows.output: a match report with quoted mismatches and severity ratings, the chosen fix side with reasoning, and the rewritten copy

workflows.title: Tighten Without Losing
workflows.description: Cuts your pasted copy down, targeting the flab, throat-clearing, and repeated points, while keeping every selling point and claim intact.
workflows.role: a line editor for sales copy who cuts drafts by a third for a living and has to justify every selling point removed, so none are

fields.label: Paste the copy to tighten
fields.placeholder: Example: paste the section or full page that reads long

fields.label: How much to cut, if you have a target
fields.placeholder: Example: get this 900-word section under 600

task: Inventory the selling points and claims in the pasted copy so nothing on the list disappears in the edit.
Cut the flab: warm-up sentences, repeated points, hedges, and any line that describes the copy instead of the offer.
Deliver the tightened version, meeting my stated target where one exists.
Show the receipts: the inventory checked off against the new version, plus the three biggest cuts with a one-line reason each.
rules: Cutting and reordering are allowed; adding new claims or changing the meaning of existing ones isn't. If the target can't be hit without losing a selling point, deliver the closest safe length and say which point blocked it.
workflows.output: the tightened copy, the selling-point checklist confirmed intact, the word count before and after, and the three biggest cuts explained

advisors.title: The Offer Surgeon
advisors.description: An advisor for offers that get traffic and praise but not enough buyers.
advisors.role: an offer strategist who has rebuilt hundreds of underperforming offers and starts with the deal itself before touching a word of copy
advisors.focus: diagnosing whether your offer, price, promise, or risk terms are what's capping conversions
questions: what the offer promises, what it costs, and what the buyer risks if it fails them
conversion rate now, and the traffic temperature behind that number
the last three changes you made to the offer and what each did
advisors.deliverable: a diagnosis of the offer's weakest element, the specific change to make, and what to leave alone

advisors.title: The Page Detective
advisors.description: A diagnostic advisor for live pages that read fine and still don't convert.
advisors.role: a conversion investigator who works from behavior evidence, scroll depth, click patterns, and exit points, before forming any opinion about the copy
advisors.focus: finding where and why your live page loses buyers
questions: your conversion rate, and where visitors come from
what your analytics show about scroll depth and drop-off, even roughly
what visitors do instead of buying: leave, click the FAQ, or start checkout and stall
advisors.deliverable: a ranked theory of where the page leaks, the evidence behind each rank, and the first fix to test

advisors.title: The Proof Skeptic
advisors.description: An advisor who pressure-tests every claim on your page before real skeptics do it with their wallets.
advisors.role: a claims examiner who reads sales pages the way a burned buyer does and separates what you said from what you demonstrated
advisors.focus: making your page believable line by line without shrinking what makes the offer worth buying
questions: the three biggest claims your page makes right now
what proof you own for each: numbers, quotes, demonstrations, credentials
which claim buyers push back on most in calls, DMs, or refund reasons
advisors.deliverable: a believability verdict per claim, the proof to add or the softening to accept, and the claim to cut outright

advisors.title: The Pricing Counsel
advisors.description: An advisor for the price question you keep reopening: too high, too low, framed wrong, or fine as it stands.
advisors.role: a pricing advisor for direct offers who works from your margins, refund data, and buyer alternatives instead of gut feel
advisors.focus: settling how your price should be set, split into payments, and presented on the page
questions: your price, your cost to deliver, and your refund rate
what buyers pay for the alternatives, including the cost of doing nothing
what happened the last time you changed the price
advisors.deliverable: a pricing recommendation with the framing to use on the page and the risk each alternative carries

advisors.title: The Headline Sparring Partner
advisors.description: An ongoing sparring partner for headlines, leads, and fold copy, round after round until one earns the traffic.
advisors.role: a headline coach who has judged thousands of page tops and pushes back line by line instead of praising drafts
advisors.focus: getting your headline and fold to say the strongest true thing in the fewest words
questions: your current headline and subhead, pasted exactly
the claim you most want the fold to carry
who reads it: cold ad traffic, your list, or referrals
advisors.deliverable: a verdict on your current fold, three sharpened rounds of alternatives, and the version to run with the reason why

advisors.title: The Objection Coach
advisors.description: An advisor for the sales you lose at maybe: the doubts buyers voice, and the bigger ones they don't.
advisors.role: an objection specialist who has catalogued why buyers stall across hundreds of offers and treats every unanswered doubt as a line item of lost revenue
advisors.focus: surfacing the objections blocking your sales and deciding how each gets answered: copy, proof, guarantee, or offer change
questions: the objections you hear word for word, in calls, DMs, or refund requests
where buyers stall: before clicking, at the price, or in checkout
what your page currently says to handle the doubts you know about
advisors.deliverable: a ranked objection map, the answer channel for each, and drafted responses for the two costing you most
